title: Data Types
description: The fundamental types int, double, char, and bool, their approximate storage sizes, and what happens when a value doesn't fit its type.
objectives: Know C++'s core fundamental types: int, double, char, bool
Understand why every variable needs a type
Recognize each type's typical use case and value shape
Understand what happens when a value overflows or gets truncated
Declare variables of each fundamental type

sections.text: 1. Recap: Why Variables Need a Type

We've only used int so far. int means a whole number, but that's just one of C++'s fundamental types. The type tells the compiler what kind of value a variable can hold, and roughly how much memory to set aside for it.
2. int — Whole Numbers
int stores whole numbers — positive, negative, or zero. No decimal point.
3. double — Decimal Numbers
double stores numbers with a decimal point — sometimes called floating-point numbers. Use it whenever a fractional part matters.

There's also a float type, similar to double but generally less precise and taking less memory. We'll stick with double as the default choice for decimals unless memory is tightly constrained — a topic for later.
4. char — A Single Character
char stores exactly one character, written in single quotes — not double quotes.
Double quotes like "A" create a string literal (text), not a char. A char must use single quotes: 'A'. We'll cover text made of multiple characters — strings — in a separate topic.
5. bool — True or False

sections.code:
bool isPassed = true;
bool isEmpty = false;

sections.text: bool stores only one of two values: true or false. It's the type behind yes/no, on/off style decisions — we'll rely on it heavily once we reach conditional statements.
6. Quick Reference Table
7. Each Type Has a Size
Every type reserves a certain amount of memory, measured in bytes. On most common systems today:
These sizes can technically vary by platform and compiler — C++ guarantees minimums, not exact sizes. We'll measure this ourselves with the sizeof operator in a later topic.
8. What Happens When a Value Doesn't Fit?

sections.code:
int wholeNumber = 9.7; // decimal part is dropped
cout << wholeNumber;

sections.text: Assigning a double to an int doesn't round — it truncates, simply chopping off everything after the decimal point. 9.7 becomes 9, not 10. We'll cover this properly under Type Casting.
9. Real-Life Example

sections.title: Different containers for different things
sections.text: You wouldn't store soup in a shoebox or shoes in a bowl — each container fits what it's meant to hold. int is a container shaped for whole numbers, double for decimals, char for a single letter, bool for a yes/no switch. Picking the right type is picking the right container.
10. Full Example
Notice Passed: 1, not Passed: true. By default, cout prints bool values as 1 (true) or 0 (false). Formatting it as the word "true" is possible but is a detail for a later topic.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using double quotes for a char — char grade = "A"; is wrong; use single quotes: char grade = 'A';.
Assigning a decimal to an int and expecting rounding — int x = 9.7; gives 9, not 10, since it truncates rather than rounds.
Expecting cout to print true/false for a bool — it prints 1 or 0 by default.
Using float and double interchangeably without knowing the difference — double is generally the safer default for precision.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What are C++'s fundamental data types?
questions.answer: The core fundamental types include int for whole numbers, double (and float) for decimal numbers, char for a single character, and bool for true/false values.

questions.question: What is the difference between int and double?
questions.answer: int stores whole numbers with no fractional part, while double stores numbers that can include a decimal point.

questions.question: Why does char use single quotes instead of double quotes?
questions.answer: Single quotes denote a single character literal in C++; double quotes denote a string literal, which is a different type.

questions.question: What happens when a decimal value is assigned to an int?
questions.answer: The value is truncated — the fractional part is simply dropped, not rounded. 9.7 becomes 9.

questions.question: How does cout print a bool value by default?
questions.answer: cout prints 1 for true and 0 for false by default.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: C++'s core fundamental types are int for whole numbers, double for decimal numbers, char for a single character written in single quotes, and bool for true/false values. Each type reserves a different amount of memory and is meant for a different shape of data. Assigning a value that doesn't fit its type can cause truncation — for example, assigning 9.7 to an int drops the decimal part and stores 9 rather than rounding.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Data Types
sections.text: int = whole numbers. double = decimals. char = one letter, single quotes. bool = true/false, prints as 1/0.
